questions.stem: Which Snowflake command displays the current active role in a session?

options.text: SHOW ROLE
SELECT CURRENT_ROLE()
DESCRIBE SESSION ROLE
GET ROLE

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The CURRENT_ROLE() context function returns the name of the primary role active in the current session. It can be used in any SQL context, including SELECT statements, WHERE clauses, and policy condition expressions.

**A** is incorrect. SHOW ROLES lists all roles defined in the account; it does not show which role is currently active in the session.

**C** is incorrect. DESCRIBE SESSION ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

**D** is incorrect. GET ROLE is not a valid Snowflake SQL command.

[USE ROLE](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/use-role)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake edition is required to replicate security objects such as users, roles, and network policies as part of a failover group?

options.text: Standard edition
Enterprise edition
Business Critical edition
Any edition supports full security object replication

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Replication of account-level security objects — users, roles, warehouses, resource monitors, network policies, and authentication policies — requires Business Critical edition (or higher). Database replication (tables and views) is available on all editions, but the broader account-level object replication needed for a complete failover group requires Business Critical.

**A** is incorrect. Standard edition supports limited replication features. Security object replication via failover groups is not available at this tier.

**B** is incorrect. Enterprise edition adds features like multi-cluster warehouses and extended Time Travel, but full account-level security object replication in failover groups requires Business Critical edition.

**D** is incorrect. Not all editions support full security object replication. The feature matrix in the replication documentation clearly shows Business Critical as the minimum required edition for most account-level objects.

[Introduction to replication and failover](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: What is the effect of promoting a secondary account to primary status in a failover group?

options.text: The original primary account is permanently deleted
The secondary account gains read-write access to the replicated objects and the original primary becomes a secondary
The secondary account creates a full independent copy and both accounts become primary simultaneously
Replication of the failover group is permanently disabled to prevent data conflicts

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Failover (promotion of a secondary to primary) is a controlled role-swap: the secondary account gains full read-write access to the replicated objects and becomes the new primary. The original primary is demoted to secondary status and switches to read-only mode for those objects. Replication then flows from the new primary to the old.

**A** is incorrect. The original primary account is not deleted during failover. It continues to exist and becomes a secondary account in the failover group.

**C** is incorrect. Only one account in a failover group can be primary at any given time. Having two simultaneous primaries would create data conflicts, which is exactly what the failover model is designed to prevent.

**D** is incorrect. After failover, replication continues in the reverse direction (new primary to new secondary). Disabling replication permanently would defeat the purpose of maintaining a recovery standby.

[Introduction to replication and failover](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: Which SHOW command lists all accounts within the current organization when executed with ORGADMIN?

options.text: SHOW DATABASES
SHOW ACCOUNTS
SHOW ORGANIZATIONS
SHOW SHARES

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. SHOW ACCOUNTS, when executed while using the ORGADMIN role, lists all accounts in the organization along with their region, edition, and other account-level properties.

**A** is incorrect. SHOW DATABASES lists databases within the current account. It has no awareness of other accounts in the organization.

**C** is incorrect. SHOW ORGANIZATIONS is not a valid Snowflake SQL command. The organization itself is managed through ORGADMIN-scoped commands and ORGANIZATION_USAGE views.

**D** is incorrect. SHOW SHARES lists data shares (outbound and inbound) for the current account. It does not list accounts in the organization.

[Introduction to organizations](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/organizations)
